methods.description: Process for collecting water and diatom samples from various aquatic environments in western Canada.

steps.description: Samples were collected from rivers, streams, lakes, pools, bogs, fens, beaver ponds and wet meadows in six regions of western Canada.
steps.searchable_summary: collect water and diatom samples from various aquatic environments

steps.description: Temperature, pH and conductivity were measured in the field with an YSI 556 Multi Probe System or a Eutech Instruments Oakton pH Tester, Model 30.
steps.searchable_summary: measure temperature, pH, and conductivity in the field

steps.description: The Oakton pH meter was calibrated once annually before each field season against standard solutions of pH 4.01 and pH 7.01.
steps.searchable_summary: calibrate pH meter with standard solutions

methods.description: Procedure for preparing diatom samples for microscopic examination.

steps.description: Diatoms were collected from all available near-shore substrata, including aquatic macrophytes, mosses, rocks, fine sediments and woody debris.
Subsamples were combined with ambient water in a single container and preserved with iodine before transport to the laboratory.
Samples were treated with 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to remove organic matter.
steps.searchable_summary: treat samples with hydrogen peroxide to remove organic matter

steps.description: After several rinses in distilled water, cleaned diatom material was dried on cover slips and mounted permanently on glass slides using Naphrax.
steps.searchable_summary: mount cleaned diatom material on slides with Naphrax

methods.description: Process for examining and identifying diatom samples using microscopy.

steps.description: Slides were examined under LM with differential interference contrast optics and images were captured using a Leica DM LB2 research microscope and a Spot Insight monochrome digital camera (Model 14.0).
steps.searchable_summary: examine and capture images of diatom slides using microscopy

steps.description: Imaged diatoms were identified by the first author to the lowest practical taxonomic unit using available identification resources.
steps.searchable_summary: identify diatoms to the lowest taxonomic unit
